title: 1: The Playground
text:
Group field theory is a beautiful mathematical idea: that the whole of the universe emerges from the interplay of symmetries. We will begin with the simplest possible foundation: defining a set of symmetries, and then building a universe out of them. This is the playground, where we set up a series of simple rules, and then see what happens when we let them run.
The ‘group’ in ‘group field theory’ refers to a mathematical structure that captures the idea of symmetry. A group is a set of elements, along with an operation that combines two elements to produce a third. These elements represent symmetries, and the operation represents how these symmetries combine. For example, the symmetries of a square can be represented by a group consisting of rotations (by 90, 180, and 270 degrees), reflections in both the horizontal and vertical axes and through the corners, and the identity transformation (doing nothing). These symmetries can be combined in various ways: for example, a 90 degree rotation followed by a reflection across the vertical axis is equivalent to a reflection across the diagonal. The group captures all of these relationships between symmetries.
The symmetries of a square also include an important concept in group field theory: they are not commutative. This means that order in which you carry out the symmetry operations is important. For example, if you first rotate the square by 90 degrees and then reflect it across the vertical axis, you will end up in a different position than if you first reflect it and then rotate it. This non-commutativity is a key feature of the symmetries that we will be working with in group field theory, and it leads to rich and complex behavior when we start to combine these symmetries in various ways.



The symmetries of a square

The symmetries of a square

title: 1: The Playground
text:
2 The group
Let us introduce the group that we will be using to build our universe. It is a relatively complicated group, so once we have introduced it we will break it down and explain what each component means. The group is

G=\mathrm{SO}(4,1)\times \mathrm{U}(1) \times \mathrm{SU}(2) \times \mathrm{SU}(3).
\tag{1}
Let’s break it down. The first part, \mathrm{SO}(4,1), is the group of symmetries of a four-dimensional spacetime with one time dimension and three spatial dimensions. The second part, \mathrm{U}(1), is the group of symmetries associated with electromagnetism. The third part, \mathrm{SU}(2), is the group of symmetries associated with the weak nuclear force. The fourth part, \mathrm{SU}(3), is the group of symmetries associated with the strong nuclear force. We have chosen this group based on observations of our universe: it was a specific choice that we made in order to build a universe that resembles our own. However, we could have chosen a different group, and we would have ended up with a different universe. This is the playground: we can choose any group we like, and then see what kind of universe emerges from it.
Equation 1 is a special type of group called a semisimple Lie group. This gives it some very nice mathematical properties that we will be using to create our universe. In particular, Lie groups have a corresponding Lie algebra, which is a mathematical structure that captures the infinitesimal symmetries of the group. The Lie algebra allows us to work with the symmetries in a more manageable way, and it will be a key tool in our construction of the universe. Each element of a Lie group G can be expressed as the exponential of an element of its Lie algebra \mathfrak{g}, which is a very powerful property to help us analyse the group. As the group Equation 1 is a direct product of four underlying groups, the algebra is a direct sum of the underlying algebras (compare to how normal exponentials work: e^{a+b}=e^a e^b),

\mathfrak{g}=\mathfrak{so}(4,1)\oplus \mathfrak{u}(1) \oplus \mathfrak{su}(2) \oplus \mathfrak{su}(3).
\tag{2}
Each of these algebras is formed of a set of ‘generators’, which are the building blocks of the symmetries. The generators of \mathfrak{so}(4,1) are denoted by M_{AB}, where A and B run from 0 to 4. The generators combine together through a set of rules called the commutation relations, which capture how much the symmetries fail to commute. A commutator is defined simply as [X, Y] = XY - YX. The commutation relations for \mathfrak{so}(4,1) are given by

\left[M_{AB}, M_{CD}\right] = \left(\eta_{AC} M_{BD} - \eta_{AD} M_{BC} - \eta_{BC} M_{AD} + \eta_{BD} M_{AC}\right),

where \eta_{AB}=\mathrm{diag}(-1,1,1,1,1). This looks a lot like the metric of special relativity, just with an extra spatial dimension: this will turn out not to be a coincidence!
\mathfrak{u}(1) only has a single generator, which we will denote by Q. The commutation relations for \mathfrak{u}(1) are trivial: [Q, Q] = 0 (because there is only one generator, and a generator always commutes with itself). An algebra where all generators commute is called ‘abelian’.
\mathfrak{su}(2) has three generators, which we will denote by J_i where i runs from 1 to 3. The commutation relations for \mathfrak{su}(2) are given by

\left[J_i, J_j\right] = i \epsilon_{ijk} J_k
\tag{3}
where \epsilon_{ijk} is the Levi-Civita symbol, which is defined as \epsilon_{123} = 1 and is antisymmetric in its indices (i.e. \epsilon_{ijk} = -\epsilon_{jik}, and \epsilon_{iij} = 0). As not all commutation relations are zero the algebra \mathfrak{su}(2) is non-abelian. The commutation relations for \mathfrak{su}(3) are more complicated, but they have a similar structure to those of \mathfrak{su}(2), built from generators T_a where a runs from 1 to 8.

title: 1: The Playground
About
text: This blog is written by Tom Whitehead. I am the Head of Machine Learning at Intellegens, where we develop and deploy a machine learning toolkit for experimental design in the materials, chemicals, formulations, and life sciences industry. I have a background in physics and machine learning, and enjoy solving and writing about complex problems in these fields.

title: Beginner’s Guide to Group Field Theory
text:
Welcome to this set of posts discussing group field theory! This is a topic in theoretical physics that aims to build a picture of the universe starting from fundamental symmetries and interactions. The idea is to use mathematical structures called groups to describe the symmetries of physical systems, and then to build up to a realistic picture of our universe from there.
This is a beginner’s guide, both through aiming to be accessible to those without a full background in the topic, and through this being a relatively new area for me! I am keen to extend my understanding of the topic, and to share that understanding with others.














2: Representations

title: 2: Representations
text:
In the last post we looked at groups, algebras, and Casimir invariants. This time, we’re going to dive deep into the world of representation theory, and begin connecting the abstract mathematical structures of groups and algebras to the physical properties of particles in our universe.
A ‘representation’ of an algebra is a way of expressing the elements of the algebra as matrices, and the algebra operation as matrix multiplication. There are generally multiple different representations of a given algebra, and each representation can be thought of as a different way of realizing the symmetries of the algebra.
Let’s walk through the details of how to build representations using the example of the algebra \mathfrak{su}(2), which is part of the full algebra we are looking at. First, we need to explore the structure of \mathfrak{su}(2) a bit more, and introduce the Cartan-Weyl basis of the algebra.


We discussed last time that \mathfrak{su}(2) has three generators J_i and a single Casimir invariant \mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{su}(2)} = J_i J_i. The presence of a single Casimir invariant means that the representations of \mathfrak{su}(2) are classified by a single number, which we will call j. This number is related to the eigenvalue of the Casimir invariant in the representation. The single Casimir invariant also means that there is a single-element subalgebra H of \mathfrak{su}(2), called the Cartan subalgebra, which is an abelian subalgebra. For the sake of definiteness we will denote it by H=J_3. We can now carry out a mathematical trick, and make some more operators: E^+=J_1 + i J_2 and E^-=J_1 - i J_2. Plugging these definitions into the commutation relations for \mathfrak{su}(2), we find that [H, E^\pm] = \pm E^\pm and [E^+, E^-] = 2H. This is the Cartan-Weyl basis of the algebra: it consists of the Cartan subalgebra H, and the raising and lowering operators E^+ and E^-, whose names will make sense shortly.
We are going to assume that the representation of \mathfrak{su}(2) we are looking at is finite-dimensional, which means that the matrices that represent the generators are finite in size. Let’s assume that we have vector space V on which the generators of \mathfrak{su}(2) act. We can find a vector \ket{v} in this space which is an eigenvector of H, so H\ket{v}=v \ket{v} for some eigenvalue v. We call v the ‘weight’ of the vector \ket{v}. Now let’s act on \ket{v} with the raising operator E^+, and measure the weight of this new vector using H:

H(E^+\ket{v}) = (E^+H + [H, E^+])\ket{v} = (E^+v + E^+)\ket{v} = (v + 1) E^+\ket{v} = (v+1)\ket{v+1}.

So E^+ has created a new vector with weight v+1 (hence the name ‘raising operator’). Note that the weight has increased by exactly 1: it is not a continuous change, but discrete. We could keep applying E^+ to generate vectors with higher and higher weight; but we are assuming that the representation is finite-dimensional, so at some point we must reach a vector \ket{j} such that E^+ \ket{j} = 0. Let us call the eigenvalue of H on this vector j, so H\ket{j} = j \ket{j}. We can now apply the lowering operator E^- to generate vectors with lower and lower weight (again going down in steps of 1), until we reach a vector \ket{j_\mathrm{min}} such that E^- \ket{j_\mathrm{min}} = 0 (the series must terminate because we assume a finite-dimensional representation). In the box below we prove that j_\mathrm{min}=-j, and hence there are exactly 2j+1 vectors in the representation, with weights ranging from -j to j in integer steps.






NoteProof that j_\mathrm{min}=-j





We know two things about the vector \ket{j}: it has weight j, so H\ket{j} = j \ket{j}, and it is annihilated by the raising operator, so E^+ \ket{j} = 0. Let’s try applying the lowering operator E^- to \ket{j}, and then going back up with E^+: 
E^+ (E^- \ket{j}) = ([E^+, E^-] + E^- E^+)\ket{j} = (2H + 0)\ket{j} = 2j \ket{j}.

We can do something similar with an arbitrary vector \ket{m} which is the result of applying the lowering operator j-m times to \ket{j}, so \ket{m} = (E^-)^{j-m} \ket{j}: let us first define the eigenvalue of E^+E^- on this vector as E^+E^- \ket{m} = f(m) \ket{m}. Now let us consider the vector \ket{m-1} = E^- \ket{m}, and apply E^+E^- to it:

\begin{aligned}
E^+E^-\ket{m-1} &= f(m-1)\ket{m-1} = E^+E^- E^- \ket{m} \\
&= ( E^- E^+ + 2H) E^- \ket{m} \\
&= E^- f(m)\ket{m} + 2(m-1)E^- \ket{m} \\
&= (f(m) + 2(m-1)) \ket{m-1}.
\end{aligned}
 so 
f(m-1) = f(m) + 2(m-1).
 This is an arithmetic progression: with the starting point f(j)=2j we can solve it to find that f(m)=j(j+1)-m(m-1). Now we know that f(j_\mathrm{min})=0 (because E^- \ket{j_\mathrm{min}} = 0), so we can solve for j_\mathrm{min} to find that j_\mathrm{min}=-j. \blacksquare



Now to connect to some physics. The number j is called the ‘spin’ of the representation, and in order for 2j+1 to be an integer, j can take on any non-negative half-integer value (i.e. 0, \frac{1}{2}, 1, \frac{3}{2}, 2, \ldots). This is exactly the spin associated with all the particles of the Standard Model.



The Cartan-Weyl basis and the method of raising and lowering operators can be applied to any simple Lie algebra, including \mathfrak{su}(3). Because \mathfrak{su}(3) has two Casimir invariants, its representations are classified by two integers, which we can call p and q. The raising and lowering operators of \mathfrak{su}(3) come in pairs, and they raise and lower the weights in a two-dimensional weight space. The representations of \mathfrak{su}(3) are more complicated than those of \mathfrak{su}(2), but they can still be constructed using the same principles. The representation labels (p, q) are both integers, rather than half-integers, but this is just a normalization convention: the spin of \mathfrak{su}(2) is a half-integer because we have normalized the generators such that the raising and lowering operators change the weight by 1, whereas for \mathfrak{su}(3) we have normalized the generators such that the raising and lowering operators change the weights by 1 in a two-dimensional weight space. The representations of \mathfrak{su}(3) are important in particle physics, as they correspond to the different types of quarks and gluons in the Standard Model.
As an abelian algebra, \mathfrak{u}(1) has only one-dimensional representations, which are classified by a single integer (or half-integer) charge Y.
The representations of \mathfrak{so}(4,1) are more complicated. Because the algebra is non-compact, its representations are infinite-dimensional. We can split the two labels (as there are two Casimir invariants) into a continuous label \nu (which will end up related to mass) and a discrete label s (which will end up related to spin).

title: 2: Representations
text:
In the last post we looked at groups, algebras, and Casimir invariants. This time, we’re going to dive deep into the world of representation theory, and begin connecting the abstract mathematical structures of groups and algebras to the physical properties of particles in our universe.

There are generally multiple different representations of a given algebra, and each representation can be thought of as a different way of realizing the symmetries of the algebra.

In the box below we prove that j_\mathrm{min}=-j, and hence there are exactly 2j+1 vectors in the representation, with weights ranging from -j to j in integer steps.






NoteProof that j_\mathrm{min}=-j





We know two things about the vector \ket{j}: it has weight j, so H\ket{j} = j \ket{j}, and it is annihilated by the raising operator, so E^+ \ket{j} = 0. Let’s try applying the lowering operator E^- to \ket{j}, and then going back up with E^+:

Now let us consider the vector \ket{m-1} = E^- \ket{m}, and apply E^+E^- to it:

\begin{aligned}
E^+E^-\ket{m-1} &= f(m-1)\ket{m-1} = E^+E^- E^- \ket{m} \\
&= ( E^- E^+ + 2H) E^- \ket{m} \\
&= E^- f(m)\ket{m} + 2(m-1)E^- \ket{m} \\
&= (f(m) + 2(m-1)) \ket{m-1}.
\end{aligned}
 so 
f(m-1) = f(m) + 2(m-1).
 This is an arithmetic progression: with the starting point f(j)=2j we can solve it to find that f(m)=j(j+1)-m(m-1). Now we know that f(j_\mathrm{min})=0 (because E^- \ket{j_\mathrm{min}} = 0), so we can solve for j_\mathrm{min} to find that j_\mathrm{min}=-j. \blacksquare

The number j is called the ‘spin’ of the representation, and in order for 2j+1 to be an integer, j can take on any non-negative half-integer value (i.e. 0, \frac{1}{2}, 1, \frac{3}{2}, 2, \ldots). This is exactly the spin associated with all the particles of the Standard Model.



The Cartan-Weyl basis and the method of raising and lowering operators can be applied to any simple Lie algebra, including \mathfrak{su}(3). Because \mathfrak{su}(3) has two Casimir invariants, its representations are classified by two integers, which we can call p and q. The raising and lowering operators of \mathfrak{su}(3) come in pairs, and they raise and lower the weights in a two-dimensional weight space. The representations of \mathfrak{su}(3) are important in particle physics, as they correspond to the different types of quarks and gluons in the Standard Model.
As an abelian algebra, \mathfrak{u}(1) has only one-dimensional representations, which are classified by a single integer (or half-integer) charge Y.

We can split the two labels (as there are two Casimir invariants) into a continuous label \nu (which will end up related to mass) and a discrete label s (which will end up related to spin).

title: 2: Representations
text:
2 Casimir values
We have seen that the representations of the algebras can be labelled by a set of numbers (e.g. j for \mathfrak{su}(2), and (p, q) for \mathfrak{su}(3)). Physically it can be more intuitive to label the representations by the values of the Casimir invariants, as these are directly related to measurable physical properties of particles and fields. We conclude this post by summarising the values of the Casimir invariants in the different representations.
For \mathfrak{su}(2) the value of the Casimir invariant \mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{su}(2)} in the representation with spin j is given by \mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{su}(2)} = j(j+1), familiar from angular momentum in quantum mechanics. For \mathfrak{u}(1) the value of the Casimir invariant \mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{u}(1)} in the representation with charge Y is given by \mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{u}(1)} = Y^2. For \mathfrak{su}(3) the value of the Casimir invariant \mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{su}(3)} in the representation with labels (p, q) is given by \mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{su}(3)} = \frac{1}{3} (p^2 + q^2 + pq + 3p + 3q) and \mathcal{C}_3^{\mathfrak{su}(3)}=\frac{1}{18} (p-q)(2p+q+3)(p+2q+3).
For \mathfrak{so}(4,1) the values of the Casimir invariants in the representation with labels (\nu, s) are given by \mathcal{C}_2^{\mathfrak{so}(4,1)} = \nu^2 - s(s+1) + \frac{9}{4} and \mathcal{C}_4^{\mathfrak{so}(4,1)} = s(s+1)(\nu^2 - \frac{1}{4}). In the next post we will connect these values to physical properties of particles in our constructed universe.
